content.problemStatement:
Mario transforms each time he eats a mushroom as follows:


If he is currently `small`, he turns `normal`.
If he is currently `normal`, he turns `huge`.
If he is currently `huge`, he turns `small`.
Given that Mario was initially `normal`, find his size after eating XXX mushrooms.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain one integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains a single line of input, containing one integer XXX.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line Mario's size after eating XXX mushrooms.


Print:



NORMAL\texttt{NORMAL}NORMAL, if his final size is `normal`.
HUGE\texttt{HUGE}HUGE, if his final size is `huge`.
SMALL\texttt{SMALL}SMALL, if his final size is `small`.

You may print each character of the answer in either uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings Huge\texttt{Huge}Huge, hUgE\texttt{hUgE}hUgE, huge\texttt{huge}huge and HUGE\texttt{HUGE}HUGE will all be treated as identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer X, the number of mushrooms Mario eats.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing Mario's final size after eating X mushrooms: NORMAL, HUGE or SMALL (case‑insensitive).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Starting from NORMAL:
X=2 → 2 mod 3 = 2 → SMALL.
X=4 → 4 mod 3 = 1 → HUGE.
X=12 → 12 mod 3 = 0 → NORMAL.
3 mod 3 = 0, the cycle returns to the initial size NORMAL.

solution.approach: Mario's sizes form a cycle of length three: normal → huge → small → normal ... Starting from normal, after X mushrooms his position in the cycle is X mod 3 steps forward. Compute r = X % 3; if r == 0 answer is NORMAL, if r == 1 answer is HUGE, otherwise answer is SMALL. This is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
